quick_facts.label: Service position
quick_facts.value: A Civil Judge (Junior Division) is an entry-level post in a State’s judicial service—i.e., a civil judicial post below District Judge. The exact cadre name, jurisdiction, and whether the officer is also assigned Judicial Magistrate functions depend on the State’s rules and posting orders.

quick_facts.label: Constitutional appointment framework
quick_facts.value: For judicial-service appointments other than District Judges, Article 234 provides for appointment by the State Governor under rules made after consultation with the State Public Service Commission and the relevant High Court; Article 235 places control over subordinate courts and officers below District Judge with the High Court.

quick_facts.label: Typical entry credential
quick_facts.value: A law degree is a core eligibility requirement in State-specific recruitment rules. For example, the Punjab Civil Service (Judicial Branch) rules applicable through the Punjab and Haryana High Court require an LL.B. from a university established by law and approved/recognised by the Bar Council of India, along with basic computer-application knowledge.

quick_facts.label: Selection route
quick_facts.value: Entry is ordinarily through the relevant State Judicial Services / PCS-J recruitment process, rather than one national examination. The governing State rules and recruitment notice determine the examination stages, syllabus, eligibility, reservation, vacancies, and timetable.

quick_facts.label: Training and departmental requirements
quick_facts.value: Requirements after selection are State-specific. As one current official example, Haryana’s rules require every Civil Judge (Junior Division) to undergo at least one year of training and require selected candidates to pass a departmental examination within two years of joining, subject to the High Court’s stated powers regarding exemption or extension.

sections.heading: What the role does
paragraphs: A Civil Judge (Junior Division) presides over civil matters assigned within the court’s territorial and pecuniary jurisdiction, applies procedural and substantive law, records and evaluates material placed before the court, and delivers judicial orders, judgments, and decrees. The Code of Civil Procedure provides that civil suits are instituted in the lowest-grade court competent to try them, while jurisdiction remains subject to statutory limits; consequently, the exact civil docket is determined by the applicable State law and local allocation of work.

In some State cadres, an entry-level judicial officer may also be required to work as a Judicial Magistrate or in both civil-judge and judicial-magistrate capacities. This is not a uniform national job allocation: for example, Haryana’s rules expressly permit this arrangement, and the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita permits a High Court to confer Judicial Magistrate powers on a member of the State judicial service functioning as a civil-court judge.
bullets: Conduct proceedings fairly and independently, including managing hearings and issuing reasoned judicial decisions within assigned jurisdiction.
Apply civil procedure and the relevant substantive laws; jurisdiction and case types vary by State, court establishment, and posting.
Where empowered and posted accordingly, perform Judicial Magistrate functions in addition to civil-court work.

sections.heading: How entry into the cadre works
paragraphs: The canonical route is qualifying the Judicial Services Examination conducted under the particular State’s constitutional and service-rule framework. Article 234 makes this a State appointment process undertaken under rules made by the Governor after consultation with the State Public Service Commission and the High Court; it should therefore not be treated as a single, nationwide PCS-J syllabus or calendar.

Applicants should read the applicable State notification and rules in the recruitment year. A representative official rule set for the Punjab/Haryana judicial branch requires an LL.B. recognised by the Bar Council of India and basic computer knowledge. Its published examination syllabus includes civil-law papers, criminal-law material, English composition, a State-language paper, and viva voce; other States can prescribe materially different papers, language requirements, age limits, eligibility conditions, and stages.

Selection does not itself end professional formation. For example, Haryana’s current rules provide for a minimum one-year training period for each Civil Judge (Junior Division), and a departmental examination requirement after joining. Newly appointed Civil Judges in Uttar Pradesh have also been formally directed for induction and field training, illustrating the practical importance of post-appointment training.
bullets: Obtain the law qualification and meet the eligibility conditions in the relevant State’s live notification.
Prepare for the State-prescribed written examination and interview/viva voce, where applicable.
After appointment, complete induction/field training and any departmental tests required by that State’s rules.

sections.heading: Capabilities the examination and office demand
paragraphs: The role calls for strong legal analysis, disciplined legal writing, evidence-based reasoning, procedural accuracy, impartial decision-making, and clear communication. These are grounded in the subject areas and assessment methods used in judicial-service selection: the published Punjab/Haryana syllabus covers civil procedure and civil law, criminal law and procedure, evidence, legal-language/essay writing, and a viva voce intended to assess candidates’ personal qualities.

Digital competence is increasingly relevant to daily court functioning. One State rule set expressly requires basic computer-application knowledge for appointment, while the Department of Justice describes the eCourts programme as using technology to make processes in district and subordinate courts more efficient and to provide a unified, paperless interface under Phase III.
bullets: Command of civil procedure, evidence, substantive civil law, and—where the cadre requires it—criminal law and procedure.
Clear, precise judgment and order writing, including legal reasoning under time and record-management constraints.
Professional integrity, fairness, composure, and oral communication suitable for hearings and judicial decision-making.
Basic digital literacy and ability to work in technology-enabled court processes.

sections.heading: Court setting and institutional accountability
paragraphs: Civil Judges (Junior Division) work in the district and subordinate judiciary of a State, with postings and service administration shaped by the relevant High Court and State Government. Constitutionally, High Courts exercise control over district courts and subordinate courts, including posting and promotion of judicial-service officers below the District Judge level; the Department of Justice likewise states that administrative and disciplinary control over the subordinate judiciary rests with the concerned High Court and State Government.

The workplace is increasingly technology-enabled through eCourts infrastructure and process reform. The Department of Justice describes eCourts as a joint effort with the Supreme Court’s eCommittee and High Courts to improve judicial processes across district and subordinate courts.
bullets: Posting may be anywhere within the State judicial-service establishment, subject to the applicable service rules and High Court administration.
The officer works within an independent judicial institution, not as an advocate for either party or as an executive-department decision-maker.
Court operations may include digital case-management and other eCourts-enabled processes.

sections.heading: Career progression
paragraphs: The usual internal progression is from Civil Judge (Junior Division) to Civil Judge (Senior Division) or equivalent posts, followed by possible movement into the higher judicial service and District Judge-level roles under the applicable State rules. The exact service-length thresholds, selection criteria, quotas, and nomenclature vary across States and can be amended.

For example, an Allahabad High Court publication on the Uttar Pradesh Judicial Service Rules states that promotion to Civil Judge (Senior Division) is made from Civil Judges (Junior Division), using seniority-cum-merit. A Calcutta High Court notification similarly records promotion to the Civil Judge (Senior Division)/Chief Judicial Magistrate cadre from eligible Civil Judge (Junior Division)/Judicial Magistrate officers after six years’ continuous service in that specific 2021 process. These are examples, not a national minimum-service rule.

At the next level, higher judicial service rules can provide a mix of promotion, limited competitive examination, and direct recruitment from the Bar. The current Haryana Superior Judicial Service Rules, updated through March 27, 2026, illustrate that State-specific structure; candidates should verify the live rules of their own State.
bullets: Entry cadre: Civil Judge (Junior Division), with State-specific equivalent/combined designations possible.
Common internal next step: Civil Judge (Senior Division) or equivalent, subject to service rules and selection.
Longer-term advancement can lead to higher judicial service and District Judge-level appointments, through routes fixed by State rules.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: There is no single all-India salary figure for Civil Judges (Junior Division). Pay, allowances, pension-related terms, and revisions are governed by the relevant State’s pay rules and service conditions, and may differ by cadre, appointment date, location, and subsequent pay-revision orders. Compensation figures should therefore be checked against the current State government notification or High Court/State pay record rather than inferred from another State’s recruitment advertisement.

As a dated illustration only—not a nationwide salary benchmark—the Punjab and Haryana High Court’s Haryana gradation list published in 2026 displays ₹77,840 alongside several serving Civil Judge (Junior Division)/JMIC entries. The document does not establish that this figure is an identical current entitlement for every Civil Judge or for officers in other States.
bullets: Treat salary and allowances as State-specific and date-sensitive.
Verify the applicable pay matrix, dearness allowance, house-rent allowance, deductions, and probation/service conditions in the live State order.
Do not compare offers across States using a single quoted ‘PCS-J salary’ figure without checking the governing pay notification.
